title: SG300 Port Down — Check Cable and SFP Transceiver
description: Port is operationally down with admin status up. Most common cause on SG300 is a physical cable pull, faulty patch cable, or failed SFP module in the combo port (g49–g52).
steps: SSH to switch or open web UI: ssh admin@<device_ip>
Check port operational status: show interfaces status
Confirm admin status is up (not shutdown): show running-config interface <port>
Inspect cable — reseat or replace patch cable at both ends
For combo ports (g49-g52): check SFP transceiver is seated correctly
View SFP optical info if available: show fiber-ports optical-transceiver
Verify port speed and duplex config: show interfaces <port> capabilities
If port was admin-shutdown, re-enable: interface <port> -> no shutdown
Confirm link restored: show interfaces status <port>
reranker_text: SG300 port operationally down, admin up. Check physical cable, reseat or replace SFP on combo ports, verify no admin shutdown. Use show interfaces status and show fiber-ports optical-transceiver.
escalation: If cable and SFP replaced with no link recovery, escalate to check remote peer port status and patch panel.

title: SG300 Port Admin Shutdown — Re-enable Port
description: Port is intentionally shut down via admin configuration. Restore service by re-enabling the port after confirming the shutdown was not deliberate security action.
steps: Confirm shutdown was not intentional (check change log or ticket): show log
Access switch CLI: ssh admin@<device_ip>
Verify admin state: show running-config interface <port>
Enter interface config: interface <port>
Re-enable the port: no shutdown
Exit and save: end -> copy running-config startup-config
Verify port is now active: show interfaces status <port>
If port security caused shutdown, review: show port security
Check if port security auto-recovery is needed: port security max-addresses 1
reranker_text: SG300 port admin-down. Verify shutdown was not intentional, then re-enable with 'no shutdown' command. Save config after restoring. Check port security if auto-disabled.
escalation: If port repeatedly auto-disables, investigate port security violations or STP BPDU guard events.

title: SG300 Port Flapping — Fix Duplex Mismatch or Replace Cable
description: Port repeatedly cycles between up and down. Common causes on SG300 are duplex/speed auto-negotiation failure, degraded patch cable, or a faulty NIC on the connected device.
steps: Identify flapping port and check error counters: show interfaces counters <port>
Check interface error and collision counts: show interfaces <port>
View current speed and duplex: show interfaces status <port>
Check link state change history: show log | include <port>
If duplex mismatch: force speed and duplex on port
Enter interface config: interface <port>
Set fixed speed: speed 100 (or 10 or 1000)
Set fixed duplex: duplex full
Exit and save: end -> copy running-config startup-config
If error counts continue: replace patch cable
If NIC on connected device is suspected: test with another device on same port
Enable storm control if broadcast storm is causing flap: storm-control broadcast level 10
reranker_text: SG300 port flapping. Check duplex and speed mismatch with show interfaces status. Force fixed speed and duplex. Replace cable if error counters are rising. Enable storm control for broadcast-induced flaps.
escalation: If flapping continues after cable and duplex fix, replace SFP or check connected device NIC for hardware failure.

title: SG300 Port Flapping with CRC Errors — Replace Degraded Cable
description: Port is both flapping and generating CRC/input errors simultaneously. This indicates a degraded or damaged cable causing intermittent signal corruption. Replace the physical cable first.
steps: Check current error counters: show interfaces counters <port>
Note CRC, FCS, and input error counts: show interfaces <port>
Clear counters to establish fresh baseline: clear counters <port>
Monitor for 5 minutes and recheck: show interfaces counters <port>
Replace patch cable between switch and connected device
If combo SFP port: replace SFP module, clean fibre connector
If copper port: test with CAT6 cable, avoid runs longer than 100m
Verify errors drop to zero after cable replacement
If errors persist: check connected device NIC for hardware failure
Save clean config: copy running-config startup-config
reranker_text: SG300 port flapping with rising CRC and input errors. Primary fix is cable replacement. Check show interfaces counters for CRC and FCS. Test with known-good CAT6 cable. Replace SFP if combo port.
escalation: If errors continue after cable replacement, NIC on connected device may need replacement. Escalate to end-device team.

title: SG300 High Error Rate — Diagnose and Clear Port Errors
description: Port has elevated CRC, input, or output error counters. On SG300 this is typically caused by a degraded cable, EMI interference, oversized frames, or a faulty NIC on the connected device.
steps: View error counters for the affected port: show interfaces counters errors
Check full interface statistics: show interfaces <port>
Note error type: CRC/FCS (cable), runts (duplex mismatch), giants (MTU issue)
Clear counters and monitor for 10 minutes: clear counters <port>
If CRC/FCS errors: replace patch cable, test with shorter cable
If runts present: check duplex setting on connected device (half-duplex mismatch)
Fix duplex: interface <port> -> duplex full -> speed 1000
If giants: check MTU settings on connected server or NIC
If errors only on SFP port: clean fibre, replace SFP module
Enable port-level error monitoring via RMON if available
Save config after changes: copy running-config startup-config
reranker_text: SG300 high error rate on port. Use show interfaces counters errors to identify CRC, FCS, runt, or giant frames. Fix duplex mismatch, replace cable, or clean SFP. Giants indicate MTU misconfiguration.
escalation: If errors persist after cable and duplex fix, replace the NIC on the connected device. For SFP errors, replace transceiver module.

title: SG300 Degraded Link — Check SFP Optical Power and Cable Quality
description: Link is up but experiencing degraded performance: high latency, packet loss, or rising error counters trending toward link down. On SG300 combo ports this is usually a weakening SFP or dirty fibre connector.
steps: Check optical Rx power on combo SFP port: show fiber-ports optical-transceiver
Compare Rx power to SFP module threshold (typically > -20 dBm)
If Rx power trending down: clean fibre connector with lint-free wipe
Check fibre bend radius — no sharp bends or kinks
Test with a known-good short fibre patch
Check error counter trend: show interfaces counters errors
If copper port: test cable with Fluke tester or replace with CAT6
Check for EMI sources near cable run (UPS, motors, fluorescent lighting)
Replace SFP module if optical power is below threshold after cleaning
Monitor link quality after remediation: show interfaces status
reranker_text: SG300 link degraded with rising errors or packet loss. Check optical Rx power with show fiber-ports optical-transceiver. Clean fibre connectors. Replace SFP if below threshold. Test copper with CAT6.
escalation: If optical power is within spec but errors persist, escalate to remote peer — the issue may be on the far-end SFP or fibre span.

title: SG300 LAG / Port-Channel Down — Restore Member Links
description: A Link Aggregation Group (LAG / port-channel) is down because all member ports have failed. On SG300, LAG is configured via the Port Trunking feature. Restore at least one member link to bring the LAG up.
steps: Identify LAG and its member ports: show port-channel
Check LACP status if dynamic LAG: show lacp statistics
Check each member port status: show interfaces status
For each down member port: check cable, SFP, and admin status
Re-enable any admin-shutdown member: interface <port> -> no shutdown
Verify LACP mode matches remote peer (active/passive): show running-config interface <port>
If static LAG (no LACP): verify same ports are configured as trunk members on remote switch
Check remote peer switch LAG configuration for consistency
Once member link recovers, verify LAG comes up: show port-channel
Confirm traffic flows: check MAC table on LAG interface
reranker_text: SG300 LAG down. Use show port-channel and show lacp statistics to identify failed members. Restore physical link on member ports. Verify LACP active/passive mode matches peer. Check cable and admin state on each member.
escalation: If all members are physically up but LAG stays down, LACP negotiation is failing — check LACP system priority and port priority on both switches.

title: SG300 LAG Degraded — Restore Failed Member Port
description: LAG is up but operating with fewer active members than configured, reducing available bandwidth. Find and restore the failed member port.
steps: Check LAG member status: show port-channel
Identify which member port is down: show lacp statistics
Check physical status of the down member: show interfaces status <port>
Inspect cable on the failed member port
Re-enable if admin-shutdown: interface <port> -> no shutdown
Verify LACP negotiation after link restores: show lacp statistics
Confirm LAG shows all members active: show port-channel
Check bandwidth utilisation returns to expected level
reranker_text: SG300 LAG degraded with one or more members down. Use show port-channel and show lacp statistics to find the failed member. Restore physical link and verify LACP renegotiates. Check cable and admin state.
escalation: If member is physically up but not rejoining LAG, check for LACP timeout or mismatched configuration on remote peer.

title: SG300 STP Loop / TCN Storm — Enable BPDU Guard and PortFast
description: Spanning Tree topology change notifications (TCN) are flooding the network, causing MAC table flushes and broadcast storms. On SG300, enable BPDU Guard on access ports and STP PortFast to stabilise the topology.
steps: Check STP status and root bridge: show spanning-tree
Identify ports receiving unexpected BPDUs: show spanning-tree detail
Check topology change count and source: show spanning-tree active
Identify the rogue device or loop: show mac address-table dynamic
Disconnect suspected loop-causing device or cable immediately
Enable BPDU Guard on all access ports (end-device ports only): spanning-tree bpduguard enable
Enable STP PortFast on access ports: spanning-tree portfast
For uplink/trunk ports do NOT enable PortFast or BPDU Guard
Enable Root Guard on ports that should not become root: spanning-tree guard root
Verify STP has stabilised: show spanning-tree (check no port in BLK state unexpectedly)
Enable storm control on access ports: storm-control broadcast level 10
Save configuration: copy running-config startup-config
reranker_text: SG300 STP loop causing TCN storm and broadcast flooding. Disconnect loop source. Enable BPDU Guard and PortFast on access ports. Enable Root Guard on uplinks. Use show spanning-tree to verify stabilisation.
escalation: If loop cannot be isolated, progressively disable access ports until topology stabilises, then reconnect one at a time to find the loop source.

title: SG300 One-Way Traffic — Check VLAN Membership and Trunk Configuration
description: Traffic flows in only one direction on a port or LAG. On SG300 this is most commonly a VLAN membership mismatch between trunk ports or a misconfigured access VLAN. Rx-only or Tx-only packet counters confirm the fault.
steps: Check Rx and Tx packet counters for asymmetry: show interfaces counters <port>
Verify VLAN membership of port: show interfaces switchport <port>
Check trunk allowed VLANs: show running-config interface <port>
Compare VLAN config with remote peer switch port
Verify VLANs exist in VLAN database: show vlan
If access port VLAN mismatch: correct VLAN assignment
Enter interface config: interface <port>
Set correct access VLAN: switchport access vlan <id>
If trunk: verify allowed VLAN list matches remote: switchport trunk allowed vlan add <id>
Check if VLAN is active on trunk to uplink switch
Save config: copy running-config startup-config
Verify bidirectional traffic: show interfaces counters <port>
reranker_text: SG300 one-way traffic. Check show interfaces counters for Rx/Tx asymmetry. Verify VLAN membership with show interfaces switchport. Correct access VLAN or trunk allowed VLAN list. Ensure VLAN exists in database.
escalation: If VLAN config is correct on both switches, check for ACL or storm-control policy blocking one direction.

title: SG300 CPU Overload — Identify and Mitigate Control-Plane Storm
description: SG300 switch CPU is running at high utilisation. Unlike IOS-XE, SG300 has no routing protocol processes. High CPU on SG300 is almost always caused by a broadcast/multicast storm, excessive STP TCNs, or a management polling storm (SNMP).
steps: Check CPU utilisation: show processes cpu
Check for broadcast storm on ports: show interfaces counters
Identify ports with highest broadcast/multicast rates
Enable storm control on high-traffic access ports: storm-control broadcast level 10
Enable multicast storm control: storm-control multicast level 20
Check for STP TCN storms causing MAC flushes: show spanning-tree
If STP is causing CPU spike: enable BPDU Guard on access ports
Review SNMP polling interval — reduce frequency if NMS is polling aggressively
Check for management access storms: show log | include SNMP
Temporarily disable SNMP if overload is confirmed: no snmp-server
If CPU remains high: reload the switch during maintenance window
Save configuration before reload: copy running-config startup-config
reranker_text: SG300 CPU overload. Unlike IOS-XE, no BGP/OSPF processes exist. Check broadcast/multicast storm with show interfaces counters. Enable storm-control. Check STP TCNs. Reduce SNMP polling. Reload if unresponsive.
escalation: If storm control and STP fixes do not reduce CPU, schedule emergency maintenance window for switch reload and firmware upgrade.

title: SG300 Memory Exhaustion — Clear CAM Table and Upgrade Firmware
description: SG300 memory is exhausted. Primary cause is a full MAC address (CAM) table due to a MAC flooding attack, or accumulated memory leak fixed in a later firmware version. SG300 has 8 MB flash — firmware upgrades fix known leaks.
steps: Check MAC address table utilisation: show mac address-table count
If CAM table full: clear dynamic entries: clear mac address-table dynamic
Check for MAC flooding: look for single port with hundreds of MACs
Enable port security to limit MACs per port: port security max-addresses 5 (on access ports)
Enable port security action: port security action discard
Check current firmware version: show version
Compare with latest available firmware on Cisco SMB support page
If firmware is outdated: download latest from cisco.com/go/sg300firmware
Upgrade via web UI: Administration -> File Management -> Firmware Upgrade
After upgrade, reload switch: reload
Monitor memory after reload: show processes memory
reranker_text: SG300 memory exhaustion. Check CAM table with show mac address-table count. Clear dynamic entries. Enable port security to prevent MAC flooding. Upgrade firmware to fix known memory leaks. Reload after upgrade.
escalation: If memory exhaustion recurs after firmware upgrade and CAM table is not full, escalate to Cisco SMB TAC — hardware memory fault possible.

title: SG300 Device Unreachable — Restore Management VLAN and IP Config
description: SG300 is not responding to ICMP or SNMP from NMS. Most common causes are: management VLAN IP misconfiguration, default gateway missing, admin password lockout, or management access control list blocking NMS IP.
steps: Verify switch is powered on and has link on uplink port (check LEDs)
Try to reach switch from a device on the same VLAN/subnet first
If local access works but NMS cannot reach: check default gateway
Connect console cable to switch console port (RJ45)
Login via console: admin / (configured password)
Check management IP: show ip interface
Check default gateway: show ip default-gateway
If IP is wrong: configure correct management IP
Enter global config: configure terminal
Set management IP: interface vlan <mgmt-vlan-id> -> ip address <ip> <mask>
Set default gateway: ip default-gateway <gateway-ip>
Check management access list: show management access-list
If NMS IP is blocked: add NMS IP to management access list
If locked out: perform factory reset via reset button (hold 10s) as last resort
Save config: copy running-config startup-config
reranker_text: SG300 unreachable from NMS. Connect via console cable. Check management VLAN IP with show ip interface. Verify default gateway. Check management access list for NMS IP block. Correct IP config and save.
escalation: If console access is also lost, perform hardware reset. If switch LED shows no activity, check power supply and UPS.

title: SG300 Unexpected Reboot — Check Power and Firmware Crash Log
description: SG300 has rebooted unexpectedly without operator action. Causes include power fluctuation, firmware panic/watchdog reset, or hardware fault. Check the system log and firmware version to determine root cause.
steps: Check system log for reboot reason: show log
Check last reload reason: show version (look for Last reload reason)
If power-related: check UPS status and power supply connections
Measure input voltage at switch power connector (should be 100-240V AC)
Check for environmental issues: temperature, humidity
View component inventory for hardware faults: show system hardware
Check current firmware version: show version
If firmware version is old: upgrade to latest stable release
Download firmware from Cisco SMB support: cisco.com/go/sg300firmware
Upgrade via web UI: Administration -> File Management -> Firmware Upgrade
After upgrade, monitor for recurrence: show log (check for watchdog entries)
If power fluctuation confirmed: install UPS or line conditioner
Save configuration: copy running-config startup-config
reranker_text: SG300 unexpected reboot. Check show log for reload reason — watchdog, power, or firmware crash. Upgrade firmware if outdated. Verify power supply and UPS. Monitor after upgrade for recurrence.
escalation: If reboots recur after firmware upgrade and power is clean, escalate to Cisco SMB TAC — hardware watchdog or memory fault suspected.

title: SG300 Boot Loop — Recover via Firmware Re-flash
description: SG300 is stuck in a boot loop — repeatedly powering on and failing to reach a stable operating state. This is caused by a corrupt firmware image. Recovery requires re-flashing firmware via the boot menu using TFTP or the web recovery page.
steps: Power cycle the switch and watch console output for boot menu
Connect console cable at 115200 baud, 8N1
During boot, press Ctrl+C or ESC to access boot menu (timing is critical)
Select option to boot from alternate firmware image if available
If alternate image is not present: initiate TFTP recovery
Set up TFTP server on a laptop connected to switch port 1
Assign laptop IP 192.168.1.x (SG300 boot mode uses 192.168.1.254 by default)
Place valid firmware .bin file in TFTP root directory
From boot menu: select TFTP download option
Enter TFTP server IP and firmware filename
Wait for firmware flash to complete (do not power off during this step)
After flash completes, switch will reboot into new firmware
Verify normal boot via console: show version
Restore configuration from backup if available
Save: copy running-config startup-config
reranker_text: SG300 stuck in boot loop due to corrupt firmware. Access boot menu via console during power-on. Use TFTP recovery to re-flash firmware. Set up TFTP server on connected laptop. Do not power off during flash.
escalation: If TFTP recovery fails and boot menu is inaccessible, the switch hardware (flash memory) may be defective. RMA required — contact Cisco SMB TAC.

title: SG300 Power Failure — Check PSU, UPS and Power Source
description: SG300 has lost power. The SG300-52 has a single internal PSU — no redundant power option. Verify the power source, power cable, and UPS output. Physical inspection required.
steps: Check System LED on front panel — off indicates no power
Verify power cable is firmly connected to switch and wall socket
Check if wall socket has power (test with another device)
Check UPS output — verify UPS is on and outputting correct voltage
Check UPS event log for power outage or overload
If UPS is in bypass or fault: restore UPS, check UPS battery health
Inspect power cable for damage — replace if damaged
If power source is confirmed good: internal PSU may have failed
SG300 has no redundant PSU — device must be replaced if PSU fails
Check ambient temperature around switch — overtemp can trigger PSU shutdown
Verify adequate ventilation around the switch (min 2U clearance above/below)
After power restore: verify switch boots fully and all ports recover
Check log for pre-failure messages: show log
reranker_text: SG300 power failure. Check System LED, power cable, and UPS output. SG300 has single non-redundant PSU — failed PSU requires device replacement. Check ambient temperature and ventilation. Verify UPS health.
escalation: If power source is confirmed healthy and switch still has no power, the internal PSU has failed. Initiate RMA with Cisco SMB — end of sale 2018, check Cisco warranty status.

title: SG300 Emergency SOP — When All Other Remedies Fail
description: Emergency standard operating procedure for SG300 when root cause cannot be determined remotely and device is unresponsive to all management access methods.
steps: Dispatch on-site engineer to physical location of switch
Verify power: check System LED and all port LEDs
Connect console cable (RJ45 to DB9 or USB): 115200 baud, 8N1, no flow control
Attempt console login: admin / <password>
If console is unresponsive: power cycle switch
On boot, attempt to access boot menu (Ctrl+C or ESC)
If device is responding but config is corrupt: restore from startup-config backup
If no backup: perform factory reset (hold Reset button 10+ seconds)
After factory reset: reconfigure management IP, VLAN, and credentials
Restore from configuration backup file if available via web UI
If device cannot boot: proceed with firmware TFTP recovery
If hardware failure confirmed: initiate Cisco SMB RMA
Document all steps taken and outcome in incident ticket
reranker_text: SG300 emergency SOP. Dispatch on-site engineer. Connect console cable at 115200 baud. Power cycle if unresponsive. Factory reset as last resort (hold Reset 10s). TFTP firmware recovery if corrupt. Initiate RMA if hardware failed.
escalation: Engage Cisco SMB TAC: 1-800-553-2447. Have serial number (DNI143803V8 for SG300-52) and firmware version ready. Check warranty status at cisco.com/go/warranty.
